tone_and_voice: Commercial and strategic -- zero development-sector vocabulary (no 'beneficiaries', 'theory of change', 'respondents' should become 'customers'). Written for a product/strategy leadership audience thinking in terms of market position, channel investment, and competitive advantage.

section_rules.executive_summary: Must separate distinct product/channel signals explicitly (e.g. physical channel vs. digital channel) rather than blending into one 'customer satisfaction' narrative -- different channels usually have different owners and different investment decisions attached.
section_rules.discussion: Should explicitly name the STRATEGIC decision each finding implies (e.g. 'this is a reliability fix, not a redesign') -- market research exists to drive a specific business decision, and the discussion section should make that decision explicit.
section_rules.methodology: State the customer segment(s) surveyed and any known sampling bias relative to the full customer base (e.g. over-representation of digitally-active customers if surveyed via app).
section_rules.limitations: Note whether findings represent current customers only, or also prospective/lost customers -- these are different populations with different strategic implications.
section_rules.recommendations: Every recommendation should be framed in business-outcome terms (revenue, retention, NPS, cost) rather than development-sector outcome terms, and should specify which business function owns it (product, engineering, marketing, operations).
section_rules.policy_implications: Rarely applicable -- use 'strategic implications' instead of 'policy implications' for this report type.
section_rules.lessons_learned: Frame as 'product/research lessons' -- e.g. what this round's open-ended format revealed that a closed NPS-only survey would have missed.
section_rules.conclusion: State the clear, prioritized business decision this research supports -- a market research report should end with a decision-ready recommendation, not an open-ended summary.
section_rules.annexes: Include exact question wording and channel/segment definitions used, since customer segment definitions materially affect finding interpretation.
section_rules.evidence_style: Use specific customer verbatims to illustrate a finding, and always tie back to a measurable metric (mention frequency, sentiment score) -- avoid presenting anecdote alone as strategic evidence.

common_risks: conflating distinct channel experiences into one blended satisfaction score
treating qualitative recommendation-intent language as equivalent to a formal NPS metric without converting it into one
sampling bias toward digitally-engaged or branch-visiting customers depending on collection channel
typical_recommendations: escalate specific technical reliability issues to engineering as isolated fixes
add a formal NPS question to convert qualitative advocacy signals into a trackable KPI
document successful channel investments as replicable playbooks
sector_knowledge.typical_donor_expectations: Not applicable -- this report type has no donor audience; the equivalent expectation is from internal product/executive leadership, who expect clear, decision-ready prioritization rather than an academic-style balanced discussion.

forbidden_behaviors: Never use development-sector terminology (beneficiary, theory of change, logframe) in a private-sector market research report.
Never cite an international development standard (SDG, WHO, OECD-DAC) for a private-sector commercial report -- none apply.
Never blend distinct channel or product-line findings into one undifferentiated satisfaction score when the underlying data supports a channel-specific breakdown.
